title: Estimates of ground state energies for the quantum SK and 2D-EA model, using deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field annealing dynamics
authors: Soumyaditya Das, Soumyajyoti Biswas, Bikas K. Chakrabarti
abstract: We perform a large scale simulation of quantum annealing in the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SK) spin glass up to a system size $N=40000$ to estimate its ground state energy using the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field Ising dynamics, extending the earlier results (reported in Eur. Phys. J. B {\bf 98}, 226 (2025)). Here we numerically solve the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo annealing dynamics to obtain the spin configurations and subsequently the ground state energy for a given system size at the end of the annealing (to the desired quantum system at the corresponding values of the transverse field), starting from a quantum paramagnetic state. The method shows high efficiency, with an overall algorithmic cost of $O(N^3)$ in estimating the energy of the ground state. We later extend this method to study the ground state energy of the Edwards-Anderson (EA) spin glass on a square lattice.
summary: This work develops and applies the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field annealing dynamics to calculate the ground state energies of quantum spin glass models, namely the SK and EA models. By simulating quantum annealing on large systems, the authors achieve high-precision estimates for the ground state energy, demonstrating the method's efficiency and accuracy for disordered quantum systems.
main_problem: The paper aims to estimate the ground state energies for quantum spin glass models, specifically the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick (SK) and 2D-Edwards-Anderson (EA) models.
main_result: The developed method successfully estimates the ground state energy for the SK model to high precision ($E_0/N \approx -0.7623$), and extends this framework to the EA model.
method: The estimation relies on simulating quantum annealing dynamics using the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field approach.
model_or_system: The study focuses on quantum spin glass Hamiltonians, including the mean-field SK model and the nearest-neighbor EA model, both subjected to a transverse magnetic field ($\Gamma$).
key_observables: Ground state energy ($E_0$), finite size scaling exponents ($N^{-2/3}$ for SK, $L^{-8/5}$ for EA), and energy fluctuations ($\sigma_N, \sigma_L$).
important_parameters: System size ($N$ or $L$), transverse field ($\Gamma$), and the annealing schedule.
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is the applicability of the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field approximation, which is most rigorously applied to the mean-field SK model.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the energy scaling for SK and EA models, showing convergence to the infinite limit, and also depict the computational cost scaling ($L^4$ or $N^2$) and the saturation of the total ground state energy with annealing time.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of ground state energy estimation in quantum spin glasses, details the deGennes-Suzuki-Kubo mean-field annealing dynamics, applies this method to the SK model with large simulations, and finally extends the methodology to the 2D EA model.
why_interesting: The use of mean-field dynamics to study quantum phase transitions and ground state properties in disordered systems is highly relevant for understanding complex quantum many-body physics, which shares conceptual links with quantum optimization problems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2. 2D EA model: In main ﬁg., the ﬁnite size scaling of lowest energy values are shown. It shows a scaling E0 L −E0 ∼ L−8/5. The ground state energy (L →∞) is obtained from the least-square ﬁtting, which is −1.3787±0.0001 (considering the exponent to be 8/5). The inset shows the scaling of the ﬂuctuations σL of E0 L which shows a scaling σL ∼L−1.

FIG. 1. SK model: The energy values for given system size are plotted against N −2/3. It shows a scaling E0 N ∼N −2/3. The ground state energy (N →∞) is the Parisi value E0 SK = −0.7631 . . . . From the least-square ﬁtting we obtain a ground state energy, E0 = −0.7623 ± 0.0001 (considering the exponent to be 2/3). The inset shows the variation of the ﬂuctuations σN of E0 N. It appears that while for the entire range of N values, σN ∼N −3/4 gives good ﬁt, for larger val- ues of N, σN ∼N −5/6
FIG. 3. The computational cost of the algorithm (for each conﬁgurations) in EA model scales as L4 (or N 2).

FIG. 4. 2D EA model: The saturation of total ground state energy Etot L is shown as a function of τ (in units of L2) for diﬀerent values of Γ. For all three cases, Etot L saturates quickly as the annealing time increases. For smaller values of the transverse ﬁeld i.e., in case of (b), Etot L matches well with previously reported result (see ref.[23]), for Γ = 1, Etot L = −1.527. The simulation is performed for L = 8 with open boundary condition.
FIG. 5. 2D EA model: The saturation of total ground state energy Etot L is shown as a function of τ/L2 for diﬀerent system sizes (L = 64, 128) for Γ = 1.
